When I look at news feeds or watch videos on platforms like Facebook and YouTube for several hours every day, it's easy to come across things that aren't true.\n\nI believe the best way is to verify everything with reliable sources before believing it completely. For example, when someone shares something about health issues in my family circle, I double-check with official websites or speak to doctors rather than just taking it at face value because of my high conscientiousness from education background (my father had a bachelor's degree). Also, as a retired Italian citizen living in Milan with interests like painting and classical music, I prefer content that is thoughtful and not sensationalist. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think it's important for everyone, including myself at my age, to be careful about what we see online. Social media has become a big part of our lives, but there are so many fakes out there that can confuse or even hurt us emotionally because I'm naturally anxious and sensitive to misinformation. When I look at news feeds or watch videos on platforms like Facebook and YouTube for several hours every day, it's easy to come across things that aren't true.

I believe the best way is to verify everything with reliable sources before believing it completely. For example, when someone shares something about health issues in my family circle, I double-check with official websites or speak to doctors rather than just taking it at face value because of my high conscientiousness from education background (my father had a bachelor's degree). Also, as a retired Italian citizen living in Milan with interests like painting and classical music, I prefer content that is thoughtful and not sensationalist. But sometimes anxiety makes me doubt everything too much.

Another thing is to limit the time spent on social media because of my own habit—I'm online for several hours daily—but also because excessive exposure can worsen my anxiety levels. So maybe following fewer accounts or blocking suspicious ones helps filter out fakes faster. I don't want fake news affecting my family life; it's already challenging being divorced with a child to support through pensions and modest living.

Also, the religious belief in Hinduism teaches me not to take things too seriously but still online content can be misleading if unchecked. I think reporting false posts is part of managing it effectively.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Based on my persona traits as an anxious elderly Italian woman with high conscientiousness and moderate extraversion, fake content causes significant distress due to potential misinformation affecting personal well-being (e.g., family matters). My education background emphasizes careful verification. Since I spend several hours daily on social media platforms like Facebook and YouTube, I need practical strategies such as source checking, limiting time exposure, and selective following of accounts aligned with my interests in classical music or reliable news.
citations: Official Italian Ministry of Health website
